Toward a synchronous quantum-hybrid machine — Part 3: a six-address quantum microcode sequencer and the visible CPU instruction pointer
Don Gunter — WodansEye Research Series — Verified design note and Qiskit prototypes — Revision 2, July 22, 2026
Companions: “A Bit-Level NAND Memory Bus” (Part 1); “The Coherent NAND Execution Loop” (Part 2); “The Rauf Quantum Local Machine.”

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Purpose and result
Part Two built a bounded coherent execution loop and a history of which cycle positions remained active. That history was useful, but it was not yet the ordinary CPU instruction pointer. The missing distinction is one every microcoded processor already knows: the machine has a micro-instruction pointer that operates the processor, and a separate architectural instruction pointer visible to the program.
In this part, the micro-instruction pointer addresses six fixed quantum control words. It advances coherently through address, preparation, decode, execute, writeback, and architectural-IP update. Only the sixth microstep changes the visible two-bit CPU instruction pointer. The six steps occur inside one submitted quantum circuit, with no classical intervention between them.
No external program is introduced yet. The purpose is narrower and foundational: prove that a quantum microcode sequencer can perform several internal control transitions and produce one correct CPU-scale instruction-pointer update, then repeat that complete cycle several times before measurement.
The verified central result is:
A six-word quantum microcode program executes several internal processor steps and updates a separate visible CPU instruction pointer, repeatedly and coherently, before one classical measurement boundary.
Four complete microcycles — twenty-four microsteps — drive the visible IP through 00 → 01 → 10 → 11 → 00 deterministically, returning the micro-IP to microaddress 0 and all scratch to zero, with no measurement anywhere in the circuit.

1. The two instruction pointers
The error this revision corrects is that Part Two’s single tape was asked to be three things at once. They must be separated:
execution history which time slots were reached, and in what pattern
micro-IP (uIP) which processor-control block is currently active
visible IP (IP) which architectural instruction address the CPU is onThe micro-IP operates the machine. The visible IP is the value a programmer, or later an external program memory, will see. They run at different rates by design: the micro-IP moves six times for every one change of the visible IP.
u0 → u1 → u2 → u3 → u4 → u5
↓
IP changes onceThis is not a quantum subtlety. It is how microcoded processors have always worked, and the reason to state it explicitly here is that Part Two’s history tape looked enough like a program counter to be mistaken for one.
1.1 A record is not a token
Part Two advanced execution with a controlled-NOT into the next position. That propagates reachability — it sets the next slot while leaving the previous one set. Tested directly:
x(0); cx(0,1); cx(1,2) -> 111All three bits are set. Nothing moved. The earlier draft of this paper described that operation as “a token moves from t to t+1,” and that description was wrong; the correct reading is a cumulative reachability record, which is exactly what made it a usable execution history in Part Two.
A genuine one-hot transition must clear its source:
def move_token(qc, src, dst):
qc.cx(src, dst) # set the next microaddress
qc.cx(dst, src) # clear the sourcex(0); move(0,1); move(1,2) -> 001Now exactly one microaddress is set. The micro-IP in this part uses the moving form; Part Two’s tape keeps the cumulative form. That difference is deliberate and consequential, and Section 8.2 draws out why.
[TESTED]
2. The six-word quantum control store
The micro-IP addresses six fixed microcode blocks:
u0 PRESENT / ADDRESS present the visible IP to the machine
u1 PREPARE stage the operand
u2 DECODE CONTROL (fixed microcode; no external opcode yet)
u3 EXECUTE ALU the reversible operation of this microprogram
u4 LATCH RESULT / FLAGS latch into the flag register
u5 UPDATE VISIBLE IP increment IP, clean scratch, return uIP to 0These names become the conventional ADDRESS, FETCH, DECODE, EXECUTE, WRITEBACK, ADVANCE when an external program memory is added. For the present test they are fixed microcode operating the processor’s own state.
The visible IP is incremented by a controlled ripple, gated on the micro-IP resting at u5:
def increment_ip(qc, enable, ip):
for i in range(len(ip) - 1, 0, -1):
qc.mcx([enable, *ip[:i]], ip[i])
qc.cx(enable, ip[0])One ordering detail is load-bearing, and it was found by a failing test rather than by inspection. Microstep u0 reads ip[0] into the ALU workspace; u5 must uncompute that read before the increment, while ip[0] still holds the value that produced it. An earlier build incremented first, and because the increment always flips ip[0], the uncompute no longer cancelled — leaving alu = 10 after every odd-numbered cycle. Scratch cleanliness is not automatic in a machine whose own state feeds its scratch; the uncompute must be scheduled against the pre-update value.
[BUILT] [TESTED]
3. The literal slow machine
The first implementation is deliberately literal and inspectable: six one-hot micro-IP qubits, a two-bit visible IP, a two-qubit ALU workspace, one flag qubit — eleven logical qubits in total.
uIP = 100000 micro-IP at microaddress 0
IP = 00 visible instruction pointerThe micro-IP travels through all six control words and returns:
100000 → 010000 → 001000 → 000100 → 000010 → 000001 → 100000remaining one-hot at every step, which is verified as an explicit invariant rather than assumed.
4. One complete CPU-scale IP update
Exact statevector result for a single microcycle:
uIP=100000 (addr 0) IP=01 alu=00 flag=0 P=1.0000Six microsteps produced exactly one architectural update. The visible IP is unchanged through microsteps 0 to 4 and changes only during microstep 5 — checked at each intermediate step, not only at the end. The micro-IP is back at microaddress 0, scratch is clean, and the outcome is deterministic.
[TESTED]
5. Repeated coherent CPU cycles
Four complete microcycles — twenty-four microsteps — in one circuit, with no measurement between them:
after 0 cycles: IP = 00 uIP addr = 0
after 1 cycles: IP = 01 uIP addr = 0
after 2 cycles: IP = 10 uIP addr = 0
after 3 cycles: IP = 11 uIP addr = 0
after 4 cycles: IP = 00 uIP addr = 0Final state after four cycles: IP = 00, uIP = 100000, alu = 00, flag = 0, at probability 1.0000. The relation IP_final = (IP_initial + cycles) mod 4 is verified for every initial IP value and for cycle counts zero through five.
This is the timing point the part exists to make. The six microsteps are not six host interactions and not six classical clock waits. They are one coherent gate sequence, and several complete CPU-scale updates fit inside a single submitted circuit:
classical preparation
↓
u0 u1 u2 u3 u4 u5 one visible IP update
u0 u1 u2 u3 u4 u5 another
u0 u1 u2 u3 u4 u5 another
↓
one final measurement
↓
classical commitThe real cost is the gate depth of one microcycle multiplied by the number of coherent CPU cycles, bounded by depth, two-qubit error, qubit count, and coherence or logical-error budget.
[TESTED]
6. Reduction after proof
The literal form establishes semantics. It is not meant to be the efficient circuit.
Because the microcode is fixed — no operand steers which block does what — the entire six-address walk is semantic scaffolding whose net effect on the visible registers is exactly one increment of IP, with the micro-IP returned to zero and scratch untouched. The reduced form performs that net effect directly, and must agree with the literal form on every basis input. It does: literal and reduced are checked against each other for all four initial IP values and cycle counts zero through four, on IP, micro-IP address, ALU, and flag.
Measured on a generic basis (rz, sx, x, cx), optimization level 1, fixed transpiler seed:
CyclesFormQubitsDepth2Q depthCXCX per update1literal1120161717.01reduced1113555.04literal1177646817.04reduced114020205.08literal1115312813617.08reduced117640405.0
Fusing the fixed microcode removes 70.6 percent of the CX count at every cycle depth, and both forms scale exactly linearly in cycles. The ratio is itself the measurement: it says how much of the literal circuit was scaffolding for human inspection rather than architectural work.
Projected complete CPU-IP updates within a two-qubit-depth budget, before hardware routing:
budget 500 2Q depth -> ~31 updates literal, ~100 reduced
budget 1000 2Q depth -> ~62 updates literal, ~200 reduced
budget 2000 2Q depth -> ~125 updates literal, ~400 reducedThe editorial principle behind this section is worth stating plainly: first show the machine walking through its six microinstructions, then prove the shortened circuit performs the same architectural update.
[MEASURED]
7. Which register encoding the machine should use
The choice of encoding is not cosmetic; it decides gate cost, qubit count, and whether a register can be conditioned on at all.
7.1 Can one qubit hold a value that cycles?
Yes for storage, no for readout, and the gap between those is the answer. A single qubit on the equator rotated by 2π/N per step accumulates phase 2πk/N, cycling and returning exactly at k = N. But the measurement reads 0.5 / 0.5 at every step and carries no count whatsoever. This is the Holevo bound in operation: one qubit yields at most one classical bit per measurement, however much continuous parameter it holds. The count can be recovered by interference, but that consumes the coherence and is ambiguous on one qubit alone — k=1 and k=3 both return P(0) = 0.5.
So any classical read is binary, and the architecture must have designated commit registers where quantum data crosses to classical bits. Parts Two and Three both place that crossing at the end of the bounded interval rather than inside it.
7.2 The cost of an increment, by encoding
Measured on a generic basis for a sixteen-position machine:
EncodingQubitsCX per incrementDepthUnary tape1711Binary6133271Phase (Fourier)501
A three-qubit Fourier counter incremented k times and passed through one inverse quantum Fourier transform returns k with certainty for all eight values.
7.3 The rule
Encode by verb. Control in unary, accumulate in phase, compare in binary, and cross to classical only at the commit.
The micro-IP is unary because it must gate its own control blocks. The visible IP is binary because it must eventually be compared and computed on. A phase-encoded count would be the cheapest accumulator, but it cannot be branched on without leaving the Fourier basis — and it is still a record, so it changes the resource curve, not the interference boundary.
[TESTED] [DESIGN]
8. Supporting experiments
Three earlier prototypes are retained. Their results stand; their roles are narrower than the previous draft claimed.
8.1 Coherent predicated routing
A condition qubit routes execution reachability without being measured. Two execution traces coexist in one state with different step counts:
path=0 trace=11111 steps=5 P=0.500000
path=1 trace=10001 steps=2 P=0.500000This proves coherent branch routing and path-dependent execution length. It does not prove that a binary architectural IP jumped to an arbitrary program address — the register being written is the reachability record, not the visible IP.
8.2 Why time and address must be separate registers
A backward controlled-NOT onto a history bit already set toggles it back to zero:
111 → 101The earlier draft read this as “backward jumps do not work.” The correct reading is narrower and more useful:
A statically unrolled time slot cannot be re-executed by rewriting its old history bit. Execution time slots and architectural program addresses must therefore be represented separately. A later architecture may re
